skill.name: clojure-write
skill.description: Guide Clojure and ClojureScript development with Metabase conventions, REPL-driven workflow, linting, formatting, and testing practices.
skill.summary: Write and refactor Metabase Clojure code using project conventions and REPL-driven feedback.
skill.author: metabase

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found a Markdown guide rather than executable skill code. Most hits are false positives from inline examples and Clojure style terms. The remaining publishable risk is that the skill instructs agents to run project tooling and evaluate Clojure through a REPL.

medium_findings.title: External Command and REPL Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill explicitly instructs agents to run Metabase tooling and evaluate Clojure through ./bin/mage. This is expected for a Clojure development skill, but it can execute project code and should be used only in trusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and REPL instructions are explicit and repeated. The context is legitimate development workflow, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Editing Guidance Is Scoped to Project Files
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with low severity: The skill tells agents to write code into files and check formatting. It also instructs agents not to read or edit files outside the project folder, which reduces the filesystem risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file editing instructions are clear, but they are normal for a coding skill and include a project-boundary constraint.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Reconnaissance Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported high-risk weak cryptography and reconnaissance hits resolve to ordinary Markdown prose, Clojure naming guidance, or style examples. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, system inventory, data collection, or malicious intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain style-guide language and Markdown text, not crypto APIs or host reconnaissance logic. There is no executable code in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Static Hard-Link Match Is a Shell Quoting False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The filesystem hard-link alert points to a bash example with complex quoting for a REPL string. No evidence found of link creation or filesystem manipulation at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is inside a fenced bash example invoking ./bin/mage -repl. It does not contain a hard-link command or link API call.

dangerous_patterns.title: REPL Evaluation Command Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents ./bin/mage -repl usage. This can execute Clojure expressions in the target project environment and should be limited to trusted workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The REPL command pattern is explicit and semantically relevant. It is not malicious by itself, but it is capable of executing code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project Tooling Command Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends lint, format, and test commands. These are normal for development but can run repository scripts and should follow standard workspace trust controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present and have clear development purpose. Risk depends on the repository being operated on, not on hidden behavior in the skill.

content.value_statement: Metabase Clojure work requires project-specific style, module, API, and testing conventions. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through REPL-driven development with those conventions in mind.
actual_capabilities: Applies Metabase Clojure and ClojureScript naming, structure, and documentation conventions.
Guides REPL-driven development using clojure-mcp tools when available.
Documents fallback ./bin/mage commands for linting, formatting, testing, and REPL evaluation.
Explains Metabase module, REST API, MBQL, database model, and driver conventions.
Encourages small tested changes with targeted lint and readability checks.
limitations: It is tailored to Metabase repositories and may not fit general Clojure projects.
It does not provide executable helper scripts or automated code transformations.
REPL and mage commands require a correctly configured local Metabase development environment.
It cannot verify project behavior without access to the repository tools and tests.

use_cases.title: Implement a Metabase Backend Feature
use_cases.description: Use the skill to place namespaces, write small functions, add docstrings, and run focused tests for a Clojure feature.

use_cases.title: Refactor Existing Clojure Code
use_cases.description: Use the skill to keep naming, private vars, module boundaries, and readability checks aligned during refactoring.

use_cases.title: Review REST API Changes
use_cases.description: Use the skill to check endpoint schemas, naming conventions, side effects, and required tests before review.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Small Function
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task for a narrow source change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clojure-write skill to add a small Metabase Clojure function. Follow naming, docstring, privacy, and test guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Write Focused Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clojure-write skill to add targeted tests for this namespace. Mark pure tests parallel when appropriate and run the smallest useful test command.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Namespace
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clojure-write skill to refactor this namespace without changing behavior. Preserve Metabase module boundaries and verify readability after each Clojure edit.

prompt_templates.title: Design an API Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clojure-write skill to implement a Metabase REST API change. Include complete Malli schemas, response schemas, tests, and module-appropriate placement.

output_examples.input: Add a new helper for a Metabase module.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan, a private helper with a clear name, a matching test outline, and the targeted lint and test commands to run.
A checklist covering route naming, request and response schemas, side effects, module placement, and missing tests.

output_examples.input: Refactor Clojure code that is hard to read.
output_examples.output: A smaller set of composable functions, notes on changed call sites, readability verification steps, and focused regression tests.

best_practices: Use clojure-mcp tools for REPL and structural edits when they are available.
Make small changes and verify them with targeted tests, linting, formatting, and readability checks.
Keep Metabase module boundaries, naming rules, and schema requirements visible during implementation.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to run repository commands in an untrusted checkout.
Do not bypass Metabase module linters or suppress clj-kondo warnings without a clear reason.
Do not add REST endpoints without complete schemas and tests.

faq.question: What projects is this skill designed for?
faq.answer: It is designed for Metabase Clojure and ClojureScript development, not for every Clojure codebase.

faq.question: Does it require clojure-mcp tools?
faq.answer: No. It prefers clojure-mcp tools when available and documents ./bin/mage fallbacks for other environments.

faq.question: Can it run tests automatically?
faq.answer: The skill provides the recommended commands, but execution depends on the AI tool and local repository setup.

faq.question: Is this safe to use in any repository?
faq.answer: Use it only in trusted workspaces because its workflow includes project commands and REPL evaluation.

faq.question: Does it create production-ready code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It guides implementation and validation, but human review and project tests remain necessary.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it instructs agents to run commands and evaluate code in a development environment.
